stories.headline: Avalanche Collapse From 3-0 Lead, Fall to Golden Knights 5-3 in Game 3, Now Trail Series 3-0
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche suffered what may be the most stunning collapse in franchise history Sunday night in Las Vegas, blowing a 3-0 lead before falling to the Vegas Golden Knights 5-3 in Game 3 of the Western Conference Final. Tomas Hertl scored the go-ahead goal at 8:21 of the third period to complete the Knights' five-unanswered-goal rally, putting Colorado on the brink of elimination with a 3-0 series deficit.

The loss was compounded by an injury to star center Nathan MacKinnon, who was hobbled after blocking a shot in the second period. MacKinnon's status going forward is unclear, adding another layer of concern for a team already struggling without its full lineup.

The Avalanche had entered the game with a significant boost: two-time Norris Trophy winner Cale Makar returned to the lineup after missing the first two games of the series with an upper-body injury. Makar had been skating with partner Devon Toews during warmups, and coach Jared Bednar had been coy about his availability beforehand. Despite his return, Colorado could not hold its lead.

No NHL team has ever come back from a 3-0 series deficit to win a playoff series. The Avalanche, who claimed the Presidents' Trophy this season, now face elimination as the series shifts back to Las Vegas.

stories.headline: Rockies Suffer 9-1 Blowout Loss to Diamondbacks; Jose Quintana Exits With Painful Left Elbow Injury
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies were routed 9-1 by the Arizona Diamondbacks on Sunday at Chase Field, dropping their record to 20-34 on the season and 6-16 in May alone. The loss continued a troubling stretch for Colorado's starting rotation, which now carries a 2-13 record with an 8.26 ERA this month.

Left-hander Jose Quintana departed with one out in the second inning after experiencing significant discomfort in his left elbow. Quintana, pitching on a one-year, $6 million deal, said the pain was 'pretty bad' and that he felt heaviness with his offspeed pitches, though he did not feel a pop. He is expected to undergo an MRI on Monday.

The injury is a serious blow to a rotation already without Chase Dollander, who is shut down for at least three more weeks with elbow tightness, and with Michael Lorenzen posting a 7.21 ERA. The one bright spot was Ryan Feltner's strong five-inning rehab outing for Triple-A Albuquerque; manager Warren Schaeffer said he would announce Feltner's status for the upcoming Los Angeles series by Monday or Tuesday.

On the same day, the Rockies did beat the Dodgers 9-6 in the other game of the day in Los Angeles, with Mickey Moniak hitting a go-ahead two-run homer in the seventh — giving the Dodgers their first losing streak of the season. Still, the overall picture for Colorado entering the final days of May is bleak, with starting pitching as the primary culprit.

stories.headline: Wyndham Clark Wins PGA Tour Event at TPC Craig Ranch, Ending Victory Drought
stories.summary:
Denver native Wyndham Clark ended a lengthy victory drought on Sunday, winning the PGA Tour event at TPC Craig Ranch in McKinney, Texas. Clark fired a strong final round to claim the title, his first Tour win in some time.

Clark, who grew up in the Denver area, has been one of Colorado's most prominent professional golfers in recent years. The victory is a significant rebound for the 32-year-old, who had gone without a win for an extended stretch on Tour.

The win is expected to strengthen Clark's standing in the FedEx Cup standings and improve his positioning for major championship eligibility. Colorado golf fans will welcome the news as a bright spot amid a challenging sports weekend for Denver-area teams.

stories.headline: Edgewater Man Arrested on Attempted Murder Charge After Shooting Outside Lakeshore Cannabis Dispensary
stories.summary:
Edgewater police arrested a man in connection with a shooting outside the Lakeshore Cannabis Dispensary late Friday night that left one person hospitalized with gunshot wounds to both legs. Officers responding to the scene around 11:42 p.m. applied tourniquets to the victim before he was transported to a local hospital, where he remained as of the weekend.

Investigators identified Anthony Joseph Saenz as the suspect after Flock license plate reader cameras tracked his vehicle to Arvada on Saturday. Officers detained Saenz and others in the vehicle, and he was booked into Jefferson County Jail.

Saenz faces a lengthy list of charges including attempted first-degree murder, first-degree assault, three counts of felony menacing, possession of a weapon by a previous offender, three counts of reckless endangerment, tampering with physical evidence, and driving without a valid license.

The case highlights the growing use of Flock automated license plate reader technology by Front Range law enforcement agencies to quickly locate suspects following violent crimes. The investigation remains ongoing as police seek additional witnesses.

stories.headline: Venezuelan Family Self-Deports From Denver, Walking Into ICE Office After Husband's Arrest and Financial Collapse
stories.summary:
A Venezuelan woman known as Cecilia and her three children voluntarily presented themselves to a federal immigration field office in Centennial in early May, seeking deportation back to Venezuela after months of financial hardship following her husband's arrest by ICE. Her husband Ronald had been detained at what he believed was a routine appointment, leaving the family without its primary income.

Cecilia, who was seven months pregnant at the time, said the family had lost their apartment and she had lost her job as her pregnancy became visible. With no regular income and few legal options, she chose to accept the government's offer of a flight home in exchange for giving up the family's pending asylum case — joining what officials describe as an unprecedented number of immigrants requesting voluntary departure.

Nonprofit workers from Housekeys Action Network Denver accompanied the family to the ICE office, though they expressed skepticism about the conditions Cecilia had been promised, including her belief that families would be held in hotels rather than detention centers before departure. The workers cautioned her in Spanish that a detention center is not a hotel.

The story illustrates a broader trend of immigrant families in the Denver metro area forgoing legal defenses and leaving the country voluntarily amid the Trump administration's intensified immigration enforcement and the financial strain of prolonged detention of primary breadwinners.

stories.headline: Colorado Ranks 4th Most Expensive State to Live In, 3rd Worst for New Business Formation
stories.summary:
New data shows Colorado has become the fourth most expensive state in the nation for residents, while ranking 48th among all states for new businesses per thousand residents — third worst in the country. The figures place Colorado at No. 47 for affordability overall, a combination that analysts say paints a troubling picture for the state's economic competitiveness.

The rankings, reported by the Denver Gazette citing Colorado Chamber of Commerce data, reflect years of rapid population growth, rising housing costs, and an increasingly burdensome regulatory environment for small businesses. The findings come as Colorado leaders continue to debate how to balance growth with livability.

The data is particularly striking given Colorado's reputation as a destination state for young professionals and entrepreneurs over the past two decades. Officials and business groups are expected to point to the numbers in ongoing legislative debates over tax policy, zoning, and business regulation.

The rankings underscore tensions that have grown in recent years between Colorado's high quality of life — which continues to attract newcomers — and the economic barriers that same desirability creates for residents trying to afford to stay or entrepreneurs trying to launch new ventures.

stories.headline: Democratic AG Candidate Hetal Doshi Launches Six-Figure TV and Digital Ad Campaign in Colorado Primary
stories.summary:
Hetal Doshi, a candidate in the Democratic primary for Colorado attorney general, has launched a six-figure television and digital advertising campaign as the primary race heats up. The buy represents a significant financial commitment and signals that the contest is becoming competitive ahead of the June primary election.

Doshi's campaign has not disclosed the precise dollar figure of the ad spend, but described it as a six-figure investment spanning both broadcast television and digital platforms. The ads are aimed at introducing Doshi to Democratic primary voters across the state.

The attorney general race has drawn attention as a key down-ballot contest in Colorado's 2026 election cycle. The seat is seen as an important office given the state's ongoing legal battles with the federal government over immigration, environmental policy, and other issues.

Doshi, whose background includes work in law and public service, is seeking to distinguish herself in a primary field vying to succeed the current attorney general. The campaign launch adds a new dynamic to a race that will be closely watched as a bellwether for Democratic energy in Colorado heading into the general election.

stories.headline: Colorado Drought Could Push More Bears Into Denver Metro Neighborhoods This Summer, Wildlife Officials Warn
stories.summary:
Colorado wildlife officials are warning that an ongoing drought could drive a higher-than-normal number of black bears into Denver metro neighborhoods and Front Range communities this summer as natural food sources in the mountains become scarce. When vegetation dries out and berry crops fail, bears increasingly seek food in populated areas — raiding garbage cans, bird feeders, and outdoor food storage.

CPW officials say drought conditions reduce the natural forage bears rely on to build fat reserves before winter hibernation, making human-associated food sources more attractive. The agency is urging residents to secure trash in bear-proof containers, take in bird feeders, and avoid leaving pet food or other attractants outside.

Bear activity in suburban and urban areas of the Denver metro has increased in recent years as Colorado's population has expanded into traditional wildlife habitat. Conflicts can result in property damage, dangerous encounters, and, in some cases, the lethal removal of bears that have become habituated to human food.

Residents who encounter bears are asked to contact Colorado Parks and Wildlife rather than attempt to scare or confront the animals themselves. Officials emphasized that a 'fed bear is a dead bear,' noting that bears that associate humans with food rarely can be rehabilitated.

stories.headline: TSA Allows Medical Marijuana on Flights, But Denver International Airport Maintains Full Prohibition
stories.summary:
The Transportation Security Administration has updated its policy to allow passengers to carry medical marijuana through airport security checkpoints, but Denver International Airport is maintaining its own prohibition on all cannabis products on airport property, leaving travelers in a complicated position.

DIA's ban stems from its status as a federally regulated facility and from city policy, meaning that even with TSA's shift, passengers cannot legally possess marijuana at the airport. The disconnect between federal agency policy and airport rules creates potential confusion for Colorado travelers who assume state-legal cannabis would be permitted.

Colorado legalized recreational marijuana in 2012, and the state has long been a focus of debate over cannabis and air travel. DIA has historically enforced a strict no-cannabis policy and has disposal boxes near security checkpoints where travelers can surrender marijuana products before proceeding.

Airport officials indicated the policy will remain in place regardless of the TSA change, as the airport operates under its own rules consistent with federal law governing airport facilities. Travelers are advised to leave all cannabis products at home when flying out of DIA.

stories.headline: Thrift Store Prices Rising Across Colorado as Resellers and Shifting Demographics Change Secondhand Market
stories.summary:
What was once a reliable source of low-cost clothing and household goods is increasingly becoming unaffordable for many Colorado shoppers, as thrift store prices have risen sharply in response to growing demand from resellers and a cultural shift that has made secondhand shopping fashionable. Stores in Boulder and across the Front Range have seen prices climb even as some lower-income shoppers depend on them for essentials.

At Pig + Pearl Secondhand in Boulder, owner Jennifer Greany has held the line at $1 to $3 per clothing item, but says the store operates at a loss and survives through volunteer support and online auctions. Greany noted the emotional reality of the current economy, describing families moved to tears when they discover items are affordable.

The shift is being driven in part by resellers who comb thrift racks for valuable items to flip on platforms like eBay and Poshmark, driving up prices at traditional nonprofits and for-profit thrift chains alike. According to Moody's Analytics data cited in the Colorado Sun report, the top 20% of U.S. consumers hit multidecade highs in discretionary spending last year, while the bottom 80% hit record spending lows — a divide playing out in thrift stores statewide.

The trend reflects Colorado's broader affordability crisis and the growing gap between income levels, as a shopping option once associated with economic necessity has become both a sustainability statement and a commercial opportunity — often at the expense of those who need it most.

stories.headline: Historic 1880 Victorian Home in Denver's Clements District Listed for Nearly $1.8 Million
stories.summary:
The H.H. Thomas House at 2104 Glenarm Place, a meticulously restored 1880 Victorian home listed on both state and national historic registries, has come to market in Denver for nearly $1.8 million. The 5,085-square-foot, four-bedroom home was designed by architect William Quayle for Denver businessman H.H. Thomas and features High Victorian and Second Empire architectural elements.

The home's most recent owners completed a full renovation that added modern amenities including air conditioning, updated appliances, and new plumbing while preserving original features such as stained glass doors, hardwood floors, brass fixtures, and wood-burning fireplaces. A custom mahogany staircase, hand-painted William Morris wallpaper, and 12-foot ceilings add to the home's historic character.

The property carries notable cultural history: it was the longtime residence of renowned Colorado landscape photographer Louis C. McClure, and members of the Nitty Gritty Dirt Band also lived there at various points. A 1877-era safe in the basement, too large to remove through any door, is believed to have been built around during the home's original construction.

The listing is held by Compass Real Estate agents Samantha Sheets and Tenzin Gyaltsen. The property is located within the Clements Historic District, which governed the restoration process and helped preserve the building's architectural integrity.

stories.headline: Emerald Ash Borer Spreads Into Six New Colorado Towns, Threatening Urban Tree Canopy
stories.summary:
The invasive emerald ash borer beetle has spread into six additional Colorado towns, according to new reporting from the Colorado Sun, raising alarm among forestry officials about the future of ash trees that form a significant portion of Colorado's urban tree canopy. The beetle, which has devastated ash populations across the eastern United States, kills trees by burrowing under the bark and disrupting nutrient flow.

Colorado has been battling the emerald ash borer for several years, with infestations confirmed in Boulder, Denver, and communities along the Front Range. The expansion into new towns signals that containment efforts have not been fully successful, and experts warn that millions of ash trees across the state could ultimately be at risk.

Municipalities are urged to inventory their ash tree populations and develop removal and replacement plans before trees become hazardous. Treatment options exist but are costly and must be applied repeatedly, making it impractical to save all affected trees, particularly on public rights-of-way.

Forestry officials recommend that homeowners with ash trees consult with certified arborists about treatment or proactive removal. The loss of mature street trees has cascading effects on neighborhoods, including reduced shade, lower property values, and increased urban heat island effects.

weather.notable: About 15 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Some people have a way with words, and other people... not have way.

topStory.headline: Trump Tells Negotiators Not to Rush Iran Deal as Framework Details Emerge, Republican Opposition Grows
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump said Sunday that U.S. negotiators should not rush into a final agreement with Iran, tempering earlier statements from both sides that suggested an announcement was imminent. Trump wrote on Truth Social that talks are "constructive" but that "both sides must take their time and get it right," adding that "time is on our side." A senior administration official told NBC News that the agreement would not be signed Sunday but that progress had been made.

The framework under discussion is not a final settlement but rather a memorandum of understanding that would establish a 60-day window for further negotiations. Key elements of the proposed deal include reopening the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran effectively closed after the conflict began, and a commitment by Iran to surrender its stockpile of enriched uranium — referred to by Trump as "nuclear dust" — in exchange for a gradual easing of the U.S. naval blockade. Secretary of State Marco Rubio, speaking to reporters in New Delhi on Monday, said there was a "pretty solid thing on the table" regarding both the strait and a "time-limited negotiation on the nuclear matter."

However, significant gaps remain between the two sides on core terms. Iranian state media and officials have said the strait will reopen only if the U.S. lifts its naval blockade within 30 days and that the channel would not return to pre-war operating conditions. Iranian state media also disputed the characterization of Iran's commitment to removing nuclear materials, and foreign ministry spokesperson Esmail Baghaei said the two sides are simultaneously "very close and very far" from an agreement. Unresolved issues include the scope and timing of sanctions relief, the release of frozen Iranian funds, and the extent of limits on Iran's nuclear program.

The conflict began on Feb. 28 when the United States and Israel launched wide-ranging strikes against Iran, triggering Iranian counterattacks against Israel and U.S.-allied Gulf states. Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly 20 percent of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas passes — sent global energy prices sharply higher. A ceasefire was reached in early April, after which the U.S. imposed a blockade on Iranian ports that Trump has said will remain in place until a final deal is signed.

The proposed framework has divided Republican lawmakers, with some warning it concedes too much to Tehran. Senator Ted Cruz called a potential deal a "disastrous mistake," and Senate Armed Services Committee Chairman Roger Wicker argued that a 60-day ceasefire extension would negate the gains of military operations under what he called Operation Epic Fury. Representative Mike Lawler of the House Foreign Affairs Committee took a more supportive stance, crediting the administration with forcing Iran into substantive negotiations. Iran is believed to have held approximately 440 kilograms of uranium enriched to 60 percent purity at the outset of the conflict — material that could be further processed to weapons-grade levels.
